content.assessment: This baseline is grounded in demographic mechanics but this lens's findings add a layer: applications data already show the beginning of a passive shift (six-year applications +11%, eight-year -14%) alongside stagnant physical capacity (F-live-school_network_planning-02, F-live-school_network_planning-03), corroborating the proposal's premise that market/demographic adjustment is already underway. However, the lens's geographic-concentration finding (F-live-school_network_planning-05) suggests this passive contraction is unlikely to be geographically uniform: urban/Budapest capacity, being older, larger, and more established, may prove far more resilient to demographic decline than rural or newer selective schools, consistent with the proposal's own stated risk of uneven geographic outcomes. Feeder-school dilution (F-live-school_network_planning-07) would also likely persist unevenly, concentrated wherever urban selective demand remains strong.
opportunities: Provides an empirically grounded counterfactual baseline against which the geographic and capacity effects of active proposals (T1-T4, T6) can be measured
strengths: Corroborated directly by observed data: emerging application shift and capacity stagnation are already visible in the network (F-live-school_network_planning-02, F-live-school_network_planning-03)
Requires no active administrative capacity to implement, since it describes an already-operating mechanism
threats: Uneven geographic contraction could deepen the existing urban/rural network stratification identified in F-live-school_network_planning-05, worsening rather than resolving access inequality even as aggregate selective enrollment shrinks
Feeder-school dilution effects (F-live-school_network_planning-07) may persist disproportionately in regions where selective demand remains concentrated despite aggregate decline
weaknesses: Passive contraction is not geographically self-correcting: historically larger, urban, path-dependent schools are documented as retaining greater selective capacity regardless of track type (F-live-school_network_planning-05), suggesting rural network nodes bear disproportionate contraction risk
